title: Fastmail vs HEY for Minimalists
meta_description: See why Fastmail fails first when senders cannot be screened before reaching the inbox and must be filtered manually after arrival and why HEY is the better pick for minimalists.
constraint_lens: Minimalists prefer tools that remove ongoing inbox maintenance and avoid manual sorting steps.

one_second_verdict.summary: Best for minimalists who want to approve senders before messages reach the inbox.
one_second_verdict.reason: Fastmail fails first because it delivers messages immediately and relies on manual filters after emails arrive.

verdict.summary: HEY is the better choice for minimalists who want strict control over who can email them. It forces every new sender through a screening step where the user must approve or block the address before the message enters the inbox. Fastmail accepts messages first and expects the user to manage unwanted senders using filters or manual cleanup afterward. That extra sorting work creates ongoing inbox maintenance that minimalists try to avoid.
verdict.decision_rule: If senders cannot be screened before reaching the inbox and must be filtered manually after arrival, Fastmail fails first.

sections.heading: Why HEY fits Minimalists better
sections.content: HEY fits this minimalist because sender screening changes more than one inbox decision. It affects what reaches the inbox in the first place, how much cleanup arrives during daily triage, and whether inbox simplicity depends on later filtering or earlier control. HEY wins by moving the decision to the front of the workflow.

bullets.point: HEY stops unknown senders before they become inbox cleanup work
bullets.why_it_matters: The user decides once whether a sender belongs instead of filtering messages after they have already landed.

bullets.point: HEY keeps daily inbox review faster by reducing manual sorting after delivery
bullets.why_it_matters: Routine reading stays focused on approved senders instead of cleanup decisions.

bullets.point: HEY changes the structure of the inbox from open admission to controlled entry
bullets.why_it_matters: That matters when simplicity depends on limiting what reaches the main inbox in the first place.

bullets.point: Fastmail can still be better when the user prefers conventional inbox behavior
bullets.why_it_matters: Some people would rather accept delivery first and organize email with filters after arrival.

bullets.point: Fastmail often integrates more naturally with larger productivity ecosystems
bullets.why_it_matters: That matters when sender control is less important than the surrounding toolset.

bullets.point: Fastmail may feel more flexible for users who want their own sorting model
bullets.why_it_matters: The tradeoff can make sense when built-in screening is not the actual priority.

sections.heading: Where each tool can break down

items.fails_when: HEY becomes the wrong fit when the user prefers a more conventional inbox and wants to rely on filters or categories after delivery.
items.what_to_do_instead: Choose Fastmail if built-in screening is not the real gain.

items.fails_when: Fastmail breaks down when unknown senders keep reaching the inbox and creating cleanup work after the message has already landed.
items.what_to_do_instead: Choose HEY when pre-inbox screening matters more.

sections.heading: When this verdict might flip
sections.content: This can flip if the user prefers a more conventional inbox and is comfortable relying on filters after messages arrive. Then Fastmail may feel easier.

sections.heading: Quick decision rules
rules: Choose HEY if unknown senders should be decided before they hit the inbox.
Choose Fastmail if you prefer traditional delivery and post-arrival filtering.
Avoid Fastmail when inbox cleanup after delivery is the real drag.

faqs.q: Why does sender screening matter so much here?
faqs.a: Because it changes whether inbox simplicity comes from controlling entry before delivery or from cleaning things up after messages arrive.
Fastmail is still better when the user prefers a conventional inbox and is comfortable filtering after delivery.

faqs.q: What usually pushes someone toward HEY?
faqs.a: Too much inbox energy keeps disappearing into cleanup that could have been prevented earlier.
Mostly for users who want stricter control over what reaches the inbox in the first place.
